Shawn MacDougall
3. Job Title
Senior Director, Disability Benefits Program Management
4. Name of ADM responsible for the program using the automated decision system
Steven Harris

Veterans Affairs (Department of)
6. Branch
Service Delivery Branch
7. Teams consulted on completion of the Algorithmic Impact Assessment
Veterans Affairs Canada AI and Automation Development Team, Enterprise Architecture
Team, Disability Benefits Program Management, Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat
Privacy and Responsible Data Division, Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada,
Justice Canada
8. Project Title
Claim Summary Tool
9. Project ID from IT Plan
PRJ-00013860
10. Service ID
1257
11. Departmental Program (from Departmental Results Framework)
Disability Benefits
12. Project Phase
Implementation [ Points: 0 ]
Page 3 of 32
13. Provide a project description:
Veterans Affairs Canada is introducing a new artificial intelligence (AI) Claim Summary Tool
(CST) to assist decision makers by reviewing and organizing the large volumes of
documentation associated with Disability Benefit applications.
Processing Disability Benefit applications, such as Disability Pension and Pain and
Suffering Compensation, is a time-consuming process. The average application, including
supporting documentation such as military records, contains over 800 pages. Veterans
Affairs decision makers review these documents to identify facts related to the claimed
condition. Demand for Disability Benefits has increased significantly, with application
volumes rising by 122% over the past decade. Increased volumes and more complex cases
have slowed processing times.
To deliver faster service for Veterans and their families, Veterans Affairs Canada developed
the CST. The CST uses AI to scan application documents, organize content, and highlight
key details so decision makers can locate relevant information more quickly. Each
summary includes direct links to the source documentation to support transparency and
accuracy, and all scanned documentation remains fully searchable by the decision maker.
When the summary provides enough information for a favourable decision, the decision
maker can proceed more quickly. If the summary does not provide enough information for
a favourable decision, the decision maker will review the entire file manually before
finalizing the decision. This approach ensures AI is used to speed up processing without
negatively impacting the quality of decisions Veterans receive.
The CST does not make decisions. Every decision is made by trained VAC decision makers
in accordance with legislation, regulations, and policy. The CST provides a structured
summary of evidence and links each element back to the original document for easy
verification.
Learn more about Artificial intelligence at Veterans Affairs Canada.
(Full URL: https://veterans.gc.ca/en/about-vac/our-values/transparency/artificial-
intelligence-veterans-affairs-canada )
Page 4 of 32
Section 3.2: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making
process?
(Check all that apply)
• Improve overall quality of decisions
• Perform tasks that humans could not accomplish in a reasonable period of
• time
• Reduce existing backlog of work or cases
• Use innovative approaches
• Other (specify)
2. Please describe (other)
The primary barrier to timely Disability Benefit decisions is the large volume of
documentation that must be reviewed. The average application, including supporting
documentation such as military records, contains over 800 pages. Veterans Affairs
decision makers review these documents to identify facts related to the claimed condition.
As a result, decision makers currently spend significant time manually reviewing files to
locate information related to the claimed condition, such as injury dates or diagnosis
confirmations, before they can begin to make their decision.
The CST is being implemented to address this challenge. The CST scans the application
and supporting documents and assembles the relevant evidence into a structured,
traceable and accurate summary for the decision maker. By automating the most
repetitive and time-intensive aspects of document review, the CST reduces administrative
workload and will help improve turnaround times and service to Veterans without
compromising quality.
3. What are the client needs the system will address? Describe how client needs have

Clients and client advocacy groups have consistently emphasized the importance of the
Disability Benefit application process meeting the Departmental service standard. Delays
in processing can limit Veterans’ timely access to financial support, health services, and
other benefits that may be critical to their well-being. The CST supports improved

_full_text:
Algorithmic Impact Assessment Results
Version: 1.0.1
Section 1: Impact Level : 2
Current Score: 56
Raw Impact Score: 56
Mitigation Score: 56
Section 2: Requirements Specific to Impact Level 2
Notice
Plain language notice posted through all service delivery channels in use (Internet, in
person, mail or telephone).
Explanation
The explanation from level I is published.
In addition, a more detailed, meaningful, plain language, explanation is provided to the
client with any decision that results in the denial of a benefit or service, or involves a
regulatory action.
This explanation must inform the client of the reason or justification of the administrative
decision. This involves a clear and client-focused description of how the automated
decision system came to the output it did, including:
• the principal factors that led to it, such as, where appropriate, a description of the
decision tree, scoring or weights of certain factors, and
• how the automated decision system output was used by human officers
The client must also be provided with the link to the published level I explanation.
Peer review
Consult at least one of the following qualified experts and publish the complete review or a
plain language summary on a Government of Canada website:
• Experts from a federal, provincial, territorial or municipal government institution
• Faculty members of a post-secondary institution
Page 1 of 32
• Researchers from a relevant non-governmental organization
• Contracted third-party vendor with a relevant specialization
• A data and automation advisory board specified by Treasury Board of Canada
Secretariat
Gender-based Analysis Plus
Ensure that the Gender-based Analysis Plus has been consulted with your department's
diversity and inclusion experts and includes:
• An assessment of how the automation project might impact different population
groups. This includes consideration of the impacts of the automated decision
system and data used in the project, as well as the likely impact of the final
decision. Where possible, cite the data used to assess the impacts. It is
recommended that the data be gender-disaggregated and include other
intersecting identity factors such as age, disability and race. If the data is
unavailable, identify where the data gaps exist
• Details of planned or existing measures to address risks identified through the
Gender-based Analysis Plus or other assessments
Training
Role-based training on how to appropriately use and explain the functionalities of the
automated decision system, at a high level.
Ensuring human involvement
The system may make decisions and assessments without direct human involvement.
Humans are to be involved in system quality assurance and can intervene in the making of
decisions by the system where appropriate.
Approval for the system to operate
Assistant deputy minister responsible for the program.
Other requirements
The Directive on Automated Decision-Making also includes other requirements that must
be met for all impact levels.
Link to the Directive on Automated Decision-Making
Contact your institution's ATIP office to discuss the requirement for a Privacy Impact
Assessment as per the Directive on Privacy Impact Assessment.
Page 2 of 32
Section 3: Questions and Answers
Section 3.1: Project Details
1. Published AIA version number
1.0
2. Name of Respondent
Shawn MacDougall
3. Job Title
Senior Director, Disability Benefits Program Management
4. Name of ADM responsible for the program using the automated decision system
Steven Harris
5. Department
Veterans Affairs (Department of)
6. Branch
Service Delivery Branch
7. Teams consulted on completion of the Algorithmic Impact Assessment
Veterans Affairs Canada AI and Automation Development Team, Enterprise Architecture
Team, Disability Benefits Program Management, Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat
Privacy and Responsible Data Division, Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada,
Justice Canada
8. Project Title
Claim Summary Tool
9. Project ID from IT Plan
PRJ-00013860
10. Service ID
1257
11. Departmental Program (from Departmental Results Framework)
Disability Benefits
12. Project Phase
Implementation [ Points: 0 ]
Page 3 of 32
13. Provide a project description:
Veterans Affairs Canada is introducing a new artificial intelligence (AI) Claim Summary Tool
(CST) to assist decision makers by reviewing and organizing the large volumes of
documentation associated with Disability Benefit applications.
Processing Disability Benefit applications, such as Disability Pension and Pain and
Suffering Compensation, is a time-consuming process. The average application, including
supporting documentation such as military records, contains over 800 pages. Veterans
Affairs decision makers review these documents to identify facts related to the claimed
condition. Demand for Disability Benefits has increased significantly, with application
volumes rising by 122% over the past decade. Increased volumes and more complex cases
have slowed processing times.
To deliver faster service for Veterans and their families, Veterans Affairs Canada developed
the CST. The CST uses AI to scan application documents, organize content, and highlight
key details so decision makers can locate relevant information more quickly. Each
summary includes direct links to the source documentation to support transparency and
accuracy, and all scanned documentation remains fully searchable by the decision maker.
When the summary provides enough information for a favourable decision, the decision
maker can proceed more quickly. If the summary does not provide enough information for
a favourable decision, the decision maker will review the entire file manually before
finalizing the decision. This approach ensures AI is used to speed up processing without
negatively impacting the quality of decisions Veterans receive.
The CST does not make decisions. Every decision is made by trained VAC decision makers
in accordance with legislation, regulations, and policy. The CST provides a structured
summary of evidence and links each element back to the original document for easy
verification.
Learn more about Artificial intelligence at Veterans Affairs Canada.
(Full URL: https://veterans.gc.ca/en/about-vac/our-values/transparency/artificial-
intelligence-veterans-affairs-canada )
Page 4 of 32
Section 3.2: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making
process?
(Check all that apply)
• Improve overall quality of decisions
• Perform tasks that humans could not accomplish in a reasonable period of
• time
• Reduce existing backlog of work or cases
• Use innovative approaches
• Other (specify)
2. Please describe (other)
The primary barrier to timely Disability Benefit decisions is the large volume of
documentation that must be reviewed. The average application, including supporting
documentation such as military records, contains over 800 pages. Veterans Affairs
decision makers review these documents to identify facts related to the claimed condition.
As a result, decision makers currently spend significant time manually reviewing files to
locate information related to the claimed condition, such as injury dates or diagnosis
confirmations, before they can begin to make their decision.
The CST is being implemented to address this challenge. The CST scans the application
and supporting documents and assembles the relevant evidence into a structured,
traceable and accurate summary for the decision maker. By automating the most
repetitive and time-intensive aspects of document review, the CST reduces administrative
workload and will help improve turnaround times and service to Veterans without
compromising quality.
3. What are the client needs the system will address? Describe how client needs have
been identified.
Clients and client advocacy groups have consistently emphasized the importance of the
Disability Benefit application process meeting the Departmental service standard. Delays
in processing can limit Veterans’ timely access to financial support, health services, and
other benefits that may be critical to their well-being. The CST supports improved
timeliness by automating the initial review of applications and supporting documentation,
allowing relevant information to be identified more quickly for the decision maker.
Page 5 of 32
4. Describe any positive impacts on clients and Canadian society that the system is
expected to have.
The CST is expected to have positive impacts on Veterans by reducing the time required to
process Disability Benefit applications. By reducing manual document review, the CST
supports more efficient decision making and enables Veterans Affairs Canada to meet
service standards more consistently. Timelier decisions can reduce wait times and
uncertainty for Veterans, supporting earlier access to benefits that may contribute to their
health, financial stability, and overall well-being.
More broadly, improved Disability Benefits application processing supports a responsive
and sustainable public service, strengthens confidence in government programs, and
helps ensure public resources are used effectively.
5. How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Very effective [ Points: +0 ]
6. Describe why effectiveness is as selected.
The CST is expected to be very effective in meeting client needs by scanning applications
and supporting documentation and highlighting, assembling, and presenting relevant
evidence in a clear, structured, and traceable format for the decision maker. Each
summary will include links to the source documentation to support transparency and
accuracy, and all of the supporting documentation that was scanned by the CST will
remain searchable by the decision maker. If the summary does not contain sufficient
information for a favourable decision, the decision maker will conduct a comprehensive
manual review of the entire file to ensure no critical information is overlooked before
making a decision.
Testing indicates that the CST can reduce the number of pages requiring review by more
than 90% and reduce the average time required to review an application and supporting
documentation by approximately 50%. The CST will, therefore, assist decision makers in
meeting the service standard and will reduce turn-around times while maintaining decision
quality.
7. Describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified.
The CST is designed to condense large volumes of material, including the application and
supporting documentation, into concise, structured summaries that highlight information
relevant to the claimed condition in Disability Benefit applications. The CST does not make
decisions. Veterans Affairs Canada has implemented several controls to ensure the CST
remains confined to this defined purpose and continues to align with the client needs
identified:
• Clear and Limited Functionality: The CST is restricted to extracting, organizing, and
presenting factual information from the application and supporting documentation.
Page 6 of 32
• Testing, Validation, and Performance Monitoring: Controlled testing, including
double-adjudication of test cases, verifies that summaries are accurate, reliable,
and consistent. Performance metrics such as time savings and reduction in
administrative review burden are monitored to ensure the CST is delivering
efficiency improvements without any expansion of scope.
• Mandatory Human Oversight: Decision makers review all CST outputs, validate the
evidence presented, and retain full responsibility for determining eligibility. If the
summary produced by the CST enables a decision maker to reach a favourable
decision, the file can be processed faster (i.e., without a full manual review of all
documents). If the file is not approved based on a review of the summary, the
decision maker will review the entire file manually before finalizing the decision.
• Feedback and Continuous Improvement: User feedback and quality assessments
inform refinements to the summarization process. Enhancements focus solely on
improving accuracy, clarity, and usability.
8. Describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you
have considered during the design of the project.
The introduction of the CST requires balancing the program objectives of improving
efficiency and reducing processing times with protecting client interests and maintaining
trust in the Disability Benefit adjudication process. While the CST offers significant
administrative benefits, there are several potential trade-offs that must be carefully
managed.
One key consideration relates to decision quality and client confidence. There is a risk that
automated summaries may omit or insufficiently describe relevant information, and some
clients or stakeholders may have concerns about the use of AI in the adjudication process.
To address these risks, Veterans Affairs Canada has implemented multiple safeguards.
• All summaries must be verified by a human decision-maker,
• Supporting source documents must be reviewed prior to rendering any decision,
• Summaries include direct links to source documentation to support transparency
and accuracy, and
• When a summary does not contain sufficient information to support a favourable
decision, the decision maker must conduct a comprehensive manual review of the
full file.
These measures ensure that efficiency gains do not compromise accuracy, fairness, or
trust in the adjudication process.
A further consideration relates to privacy and security. To protect client information, the
Department conducted rigorous compliance reviews and ensured full adherence to
Page 7 of 32
Government of Canada privacy, security, and responsible AI standards before
implementation.
9. Describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages your department expects from
using an automated system.
VAC’s service standard for Disability Benefits requires that 80% of decisions be rendered
within 16 weeks. In fiscal year 2024–2025, compliance with this standard declined from
69% to 47% due to increased program demand. While VAC improved internal efficiency,
application intake exceeded internal efficiency gains. With modernization initiatives,
including the implementation of the CST, VAC will be better positioned to meet service
standards and manage growing intake volumes.
Testing indicates that the CST can reduce the number of pages requiring review by more
than 90% and reduce the average time required to review an application and supporting
documentation by approximately 50%. The CST will, therefore, assist decision makers in
meeting the service standard by improving efficiency, reducing turn-around times and
maintaining decision quality.
10. What are your current program indicators, service standards or performance
targets?
The current service standard for the Disability Benefit program is that 80% of Disability
Benefit decisions must be made within 16 weeks for first applications.
11. What is your current performance level against these indicators, standards or
targets?
The performance level for the 2024-2025 fiscal year was 47%.
12. How do you expect the system will change performance against your program
indicators, service standards or performance targets?
The Department expects that the CST will reduce average processing times for Disability
Benefit applications by automating labour-intensive document review. While it is difficult
to precisely forecast the CST’s impact on overall service standard results given that
application volumes are driven by factors outside the Department’s control, the
productivity gains are expected to increase processing capacity. This will support Veterans
Affairs Canada in meeting service standards and managing growing intake volumes.
13. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
14. If non-automated processes were considered, why was automation identified as
the preferred option?
Partial automation was selected as the preferred option because Disability Benefit
decisions require the review of large volumes of documentation and have direct impacts
on Veterans’ financial security, access to health services, and overall well-being. The
Page 8 of 32
average application, including supporting documentation such as military records,
contains over 800 pages that a VAC decision maker reviews before making a decision. Fully
automating the decision making process would not be appropriate given the need for
human judgment, contextual understanding, and accountability in each case. In addition,
there is no legal authority under the Pension Act or the Veterans
Well-being Act for the Department to use an electronic system, including an automated
system, to make a decision or determination without human intervention.
Partial automation enables Veterans Affairs Canada to apply automation in a
proportionate manner by supporting administrative tasks such as scanning, identifying,
and organizing relevant information while maintaining human oversight and decision
making authority at all stages. Trained VAC decision makers remain responsible for
reviewing the evidence, applying legislation and policy, exercising professional judgment,
and making the final decision on each application. This approach improves efficiency and
timeliness while ensuring transparency, fairness, and accountability in decisions that
directly affect Veterans.
15. What would be the consequence of not deploying the system?
• Service quality is not as high
• Service delivery cannot achieve performance targets
• Service cannot be delivered in a timely or efficient manner
Risk Profile
16. Is the project within an area of intense public scrutiny (for example, because of
privacy concerns) and/or frequent litigation?
Yes [ Points: +3 ]
17. Does the line of business serve equity denied groups?
Yes [ Points: +3 ]
18. Indicate which equity denied groups are served and describe your mitigation
strategies to prevent adverse impacts for these groups.
Through the application of Gender-Based Analysis Plus (GBA+), Veterans Affairs Canada
considered potential impacts on equity-denied groups, including women, Indigenous
Peoples, persons with disabilities, members of 2SLGBTQI+ communities, and visible
minorities. The CST is designed to support administrative efficiency by summarizing
application materials and does not differentiate treatment based on personal
characteristics. It does not automate decisions, and all determinations remain fully
human-driven.
Page 9 of 32
Decision makers are required to verify summaries against source documents and apply
legislation and policy consistently, which mitigates the risk of adverse or inequitable
outcomes.
The primary population served by the CST is Canadian Armed Forces Veterans applying for
Disability Benefits due to physical or mental health conditions. This population includes a
significant proportion of equity-denied groups, including persons with disabilities, women,
Indigenous Veterans, 2SLGBTQI+ Veterans, and visible minorities. In particular, Indigenous
and 2SLGBTQI+ persons are overrepresented in the Veteran population vis-à-vis the
Canadian general population, according to Census 2021 and Canadian Veteran Health
Survey 2022 figures. Similarly, disabled Veterans are significantly overrepresented relative
to the Canadian general population in all age groups analyzed by Statistics Canada (i.e., 17
to 44, 45 to 64, and 65+) in the Canadian Survey of Disability 2022. By reducing the time
required to process Disability Benefit applications, the tool supports more timely access to
financial support, health services, and other benefits for all Veterans, including equity-
denied groups.
In addition, improved workload management and processing efficiency are expected to
support more consistent achievement of service standards and reduce application
backlogs. The use of modernized tools such as the CST also enhances the Department’s
ability to monitor service delivery using administrative and demographic data, including
GBA+-informed analysis. Over time, these improvements support equitable access to
benefits and services, particularly for equity-denied Veteran populations whose
representation within the Canadian Armed Forces has increased and is expected to
continue to increase into the future.
19. Are there any population groups that the automated decision system should not
be applied to?
No [ Points: +0 ]
20. Has the system been assessed to understand whether it would create or
exacerbate barriers for persons with disabilities, and are measures in place to
address any identified issues?
Assessment has been conducted, and no barriers were identified. [ Points: +0 ]
21. Could the automated decision-system be at heightened risk of being an
exploitation target for malicious actors (for example, fraud for financial gain)?
No [ Points: +0 ]
22. Have potential issues or harms with the existing service delivery approach been
raised by clients or their representatives, the media, audits or parliamentary
committees?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
Page 10 of 32
23. Indicate the issue or harm, the group that raised them, and how these have been
addressed.
The current manual service delivery approach for reviewing documentation related to
Disability Benefit applications has been the subject of sustained criticism from Veterans,
their representatives, Veterans’ advocacy organizations, the media, and Parliamentary
committees. These groups have consistently raised concerns about the significant harms
associated with the existing process, including excessive wait times for Disability Benefit
decisions that often far exceed established service standards, and a persistent and
growing backlog which has created delays that affect Veterans’ well-being by impacting
their access to financial support and health services.
The Department is implementing the CST to reduce labour intensive document review and
improve overall efficiency. This approach is expected to reduce processing times and help
Veterans Affairs Canada meet its service standard of rendering 80% of first applications
within 16 weeks without compromising quality.
24. Have you submitted a concept case to TBS and presented the project to the
Government of Canada Enterprise Architecture Review Board?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
Project Authority
25. Will you require new policy or legal authority for this project?
No [ Points: +0 ]
26. Indicate why you have chosen the response.
The Department of Veterans Affairs does not require new policy or legal authority to
implement the CST because the CST does not alter Disability Benefit eligibility rules,
decision making authority, adjudication procedures, Veterans’ rights, or recourse
mechanisms.
About The System
27. Check which of the following capabilities apply to your system.
Text and speech analysis: Analyzing large data sets to recognize, process, and categorize
text, speech and voice, and make recommendations based on the categorization
Process optimization and workflow automation: Analyzing large data sets to identify any
anomalies, cluster patterns, predict outcomes or ways to optimize; and automate specific
workflows
Content generation: Analyzing large data sets to categorize, process, triage, personalize,
and serve specific content for specific contexts
Page 11 of 32
Image and object recognition: Analyzing large data sets to automate the recognition,
classification, and context associated with an image or object
28. Who developed the system?
Your institution [ Points: +0 ]
29. Who configured the system?
Your institution [ Points: +0 ]
30. Is the system:
Custom built [ Points: +1 ]
31. Have you assigned accountability in your institution for the design, development,
and maintenance, of the system?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
32. Is there a group in your institution responsible for identifying and implementing
opportunities for improvement?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
33. Does your system conform with the appropriate clauses of standard CAN/ASC EN
301549:2024, Accessibility requirements for Information and Communication
Technology (ICT) products and services?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
34. Is a non-automated alternative planned and available in the event the automated
decision system was unavailable for an extended period of time?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
35. Is there a process in place to grant, monitor, and revoke access permission to the
system?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
36. The impacts that the development, operation, and maintenance of the system
could have on the ongoing sustainability of an environmental ecosystem, may be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
37. Describe why the impacts resulting from the system's development, operations
and maintenance are as selected.
The CST will have little or no impact on the environmental ecosystem because it is used to
scan Disability Benefit applications and supporting documentation, and to assemble
relevant evidence into a structured, traceable, and accurate summary for the decision
maker.
Page 12 of 32
About the Algorithm
38. The algorithm used will be a (trade) secret
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
39. The algorithmic process will be difficult to interpret or to explain
No [ Points: +0 ]
40. Is the algorithm (Check all that apply):
Based on patterns in data identified by the AI model (for example, neural network, deep
learning) [ Points: +1 ]
41. If the automated decision-system is model based, is the model inference (select
all that apply):
Probabilistic (infer several models to explain data) [ Points: +1 ]
42. Is the model:
Generative (for example, linear discriminant analysis, naïve Bayes) [ Points: +1 ]
43. Describe the model being used.
The CST leverages a "Retrieval-Augmented Generation" architecture hosted entirely within
VAC’s secure Microsoft Azure cloud environment. The system operates in four distinct
stages:
1. Ingestion & Handwriting Recognition (OCR)
1.1. The system first retrieves Service Health Records and application documents from
VAC’s Digital Image (DIGIMG) repository. It utilizes Azure AI Document Intelligence
to separate documents into individual pages and extract machine readable text.
This model is capable of recognizing and digitizing handwritten content, as well as
identifying structural elements like forms, tables, and headings.
2. Information Retrieval (The "Search" Layer)
2.1. The processed text and metadata are temporarily indexed in an Azure AI Search
index. When a specific medical diagnosis and associated medical code is
provided, the system queries this index to retrieve only the specific pages and
paragraphs relevant to that condition. This ensures the AI focuses exclusively on
the client's existing records.
3. Generative Summarization (The LLM) The retrieved content is passed as context to the
Azure OpenAI service (currently utilizing the GPT-5 model).
3.1. Function: The model identifies, organizes, and summarizes the evidence related to
the claimed condition.
Page 13 of 32
3.2. Constraint: The model operates under strict instructions to summarize only; it does
not apply eligibility rules, or assign outcomes.
3.3. Traceability: The final output includes hyperlinks connecting every summarized
point directly back to the source page in the original file.
4. Data Lifecycle & Security
4.1. Zero Retention: Once the summary is generated, all temporary AI processing data,
including the vectorized document content and the Search index, is deleted.
4.2. Storage: The final summary is saved as a PDF in the DIGIMG repository and the
client’s electronic case file.
4.3. Governance: No external partners are involved in the model's operation, and
Service Health Records (including those from DND) are accessed solely through
VAC's authorized repositories.
The CST does not make decisions.
49. Describe why you have not evaluated whether variables could be proxies for
protected characteristics.
It was chosen based on cost considerations, inclusion of health information and testing
results.
45. Will the algorithm continue to learn and evolve as it is used?
No [ Points: +0 ]
46. Does the algorithm consider protected characteristics to make its decisions or
recommendations?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
47. Describe why protected characteristics are being considered and any mitigations
in place to address potential issues with bias or discrimination.
Personal characteristics such as disability, age, and gender are essential pieces of
information in the Disability Benefit adjudication process. These factors directly influence
eligibility, entitlement, and assessment under the governing legislation and policies.
Because they are integral to understanding the nature of the claim, the CST must process
and reflect this information when generating a summary of the documents relevant to the
application.
48. Have you evaluated whether variables that the system bases its decisions or
recommendations on could be proxies for protected characteristics?
No [ Points: +2 ]
Page 14 of 32
49. Describe why you have not evaluated whether variables could be proxies for
protected characteristics.
The Department has not evaluated whether any variables could act as proxies for
protected characteristics because these characteristics are already explicitly included in
the information that must be reviewed as part of the Disability Benefit adjudication
process. Since protected characteristics must be considered directly and cannot be
replaced or inferred through proxies when summarizing information contained in Service
Health Records and other documentation, a proxy analysis would not be meaningful or
applicable in this context.
About the Decision
50. Describe the administrative decision(s) that is being supported or made by the
algorithm.
When Veterans Affairs Canada receives a Disability Benefit application, the application is
registered in departmental systems and supporting service and medical records are
gathered from multiple sources. Once the file is complete, it is assigned to a trained
decision maker, who reviews extensive documentation to identify information relevant to
the claimed condition. The decision maker considers evidence such as service health
records, medical diagnoses, injury or onset dates, continuity of symptoms, and other
relevant facts in order to determine entitlement and, where applicable, assess the level of
benefit in accordance with legislation, regulations, and policy.
The CST does not change these steps or make entitlement decisions. Instead, it supports
the document review stage by scanning, organizing, and summarizing large volumes of
documentation to help decision makers identify relevant evidence more efficiently. Human
decision makers continue to review the complete record, apply legislation and policy,
exercise professional judgment, and make the final decision in all cases.
51. Does the decision pertain to any of the categories below (check all that apply).
Note that the examples in brackets are not exhaustive.
Social assistance (employment insurance, disability or housing benefits, children or
seniors programs) [ Points: +1 ]
Impact Assessment
52. Which of the following best describes the type of automation you are planning?
Partial automation (the system will contribute to administrative decision making by
supporting an officer with assessments, recommendations, intermediate decisions, or
other outputs) [ Points: +2 ]
53. Describe the role of the system in the decision-making process.
The primary challenge in Disability Benefit decision making is the volume of
documentation that must be reviewed. The average application, including supporting
Page 15 of 32
documentation such as military records, contains more than 800 pages that are reviewed
to identify facts related to the claimed condition for consideration by the decision maker
before a determination is made.
The CST supports this stage of the process by scanning, organizing, and summarizing large
volumes of application materials and supporting documentation. The system highlights
and assembles relevant evidence into a structured, traceable and accurate summary for
the decision maker. Each summary includes links to the original source documents, and
all scanned documentation remains fully searchable, supporting transparency and
verification.
The CST does not change the Disability Benefit adjudication process and does not make
decisions. If a summary does not contain sufficient information to support a favourable
decision, the decision maker conducts a comprehensive review of the full record to ensure
no critical information is overlooked. In all cases, trained VAC decision makers review the
complete evidence, apply legislation and policy, exercise professional judgment, and
make the final decision.
By supporting the document review and information identification stages of the process,
the CST assists decision makers in working more efficiently and helps Veterans Affairs
Canada meet service standards and reduce turnaround times, without compromising
decision quality or accountability.
54. Will the system be making decisions or assessments that require judgment or
discretion?
No [ Points: +0 ]
55. Describe.
The CST does not make Disability Benefit decisions or perform assessments requiring
judgment or discretion. There is no legal authority under the Pension Act or the Veterans
Well-being Act for an electronic system, including an automated system, to make a
decision or determination without human intervention. All Disability Benefit decisions
therefore remain the responsibility of VAC decision makers.

58. Describe the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed
to interpret it in the context of the administrative decision.
The CST produces a summary that is a subset of a larger set of documents that is being
reviewed including the Disability Benefit application, Service Health Records and medical
Questionnaires. The CST scans the application and supporting documentation, and then
highlights, assembles and presents the relevant evidence in a clear, structured, and
traceable format to the decision maker. Each summary includes links to the source
documentation to support transparency and accuracy, and all of the supporting
documentation that was scanned by the CST remains searchable by the decision maker. If
the summary does not contain sufficient information for a favourable decision, the
decision maker will conduct a comprehensive review of the entire file before making a
decision to ensure no critical information is overlooked.
The CST does not make Disability Benefit decisions or determine Disability Benefit
eligibility. Every Disability Benefit decision will continue to be made by Veterans Affairs
Canada decision makers who will verify the facts, confirm policy application, and finalize
the decision.

104. Describe the input data collected and used by the system, its source, method of
collection, and any quality assurance measures in place.
The CST uses information that is already collected and lawfully held by Veterans Affairs
Canada. The system does not collect new information from clients. The input data consists
of:
• Standardized Forms: The Application for Disability Benefits (PEN 923) and specific
Medical Questionnaires (e.g., Psychiatric/Psychological Conditions Medical
Questionnaire, Thoracolumbar Spine Conditions Medical Questionnaire).
• Service Health Records: A wide variety of medical and personnel documents,
including handwritten clinical progress notes, specialist reports (audiograms,
surgical reports), and dental records spanning a Veterans career.
• Diagnostic Evidence: Medical reports and diagnostic imaging results provided by
private practitioners or military medical officers.
• Service Documents: Personnel records utilized to confirm service dates, rank, and
unit deployment history.
• Source & Method of Collection The system retrieves documents exclusively from
VAC’s official secure repositories. No data is scraped from the internet or unverified
external sources.
• Interoperability (DND/RCMP): Service Health Records are retrieved directly through
secure, established transfer protocols such as with the Department of National
Defence (DND) and RCMP.
• Client Submission: Applicants can also submit evidence via My VAC Account
(digital upload) or by mail. All documents received are ultimately stored
electronically in .PDF format within our Digital Image (DIGIMG) document
repository.
• System Retrieval: The CST uses the client’s unique identifier (CSDN ID) to query the
DIGIMG repository, retrieving only the specific files associated with the claimed
condition (e.g., fetching only "Knee" related correspondence for a knee claim).
VAC employs a comprehensive end-to-end human oversight protocol. This framework
mandates that qualified staff validate the integrity of the application file prior to automated
summarization and verify the accuracy of the generated summary before any
administrative decision is finalized.
Page 23 of 32
• Pre-Processing Validation (Claims Preparation): Before the CST is ever triggered, the
application file undergoes a manual "Claims Preparation" business process.
Trained VAC staff review the application, medical questionnaires, and submitted
materials to validate that the file is accurate and complete. This critical human
gatekeeping ensures that the data fed into the tool is organized and ready for
processing, preventing the system from analyzing incomplete or irrelevant files.
• Technical Precision (OCR): The system utilizes Azure AI Document Intelligence to
accurately digitize complex inputs. This technology is capable of recognizing
handwritten text and preserving document structure (tables, forms), ensuring that
older, handwritten service records—which are often critical for long-standing
veterans—are accurately captured in the summary.
• Post-Processing Verification: The CST does not replace the source documents.
Every fact, date, or medical finding in the generated summary includes a direct
hyperlink back to the specific page in the original PDF. The Decision Maker is
required to use these links to cross-reference the summary against the original
evidence, ensuring that the final decision is always grounded in the primary source
documents, not just the AI output.
• "Manual Review Safeguard" Protocol : If the data quality is poor (e.g., illegible
handwriting that OCR cannot parse) or the evidence is insufficient to support a
favourable outcome, the decision maker will review the entire file manually before
finalizing the decision.

6. Identify the consultees or partners and indicate the project lifecycle stage
(Concept, Design, Data collection and processing, Building or adapting the model,
Testing, Deployment, Production, Monitoring, Decommission) in which engagement
took place.
Engagement with internal and external stakeholders and partners has occurred throughout
the project lifecycle and was tailored to the nature of the expertise required at each stage.
Consultations were undertaken primarily at a strategic, governance, legal, privacy,
security, and program-integration level, consistent with the CST’s role as a decision-
support system.
Page 25 of 32
Concept and Design: Early engagement focused on validating the business need,
confirming alignment with program authorities, assessing legal, privacy, security, and
data-governance considerations, and determining applicability of the Directive on
Automated Decision-Making and the Algorithmic Impact Assessment framework.
Data Collection and Processing: Consultations supported the identification of appropriate
data sources, confirmation of lawful authority for data use, privacy safeguards,
information management practices, and alignment with enterprise architecture, IT, and
security requirements.
Testing and Deployment: Engagement emphasized operational readiness, usability for
decision makers, transparency and traceability requirements, communications
considerations, and validation that the tool functions strictly as a decision-support aid.
Production and Monitoring: Ongoing engagement supports governance oversight,
performance monitoring, privacy and security compliance, audit and evaluation readiness,
and continuous improvement, including consideration of equity, accessibility, and GBA
Plus-related impacts.
Engagement is iterative and ongoing, with different areas and stakeholders being
consulted as the project evolves and as new risks, considerations, or lifecycle phases
arise.

17. Summarize the findings or provide a link if publicly available.
Through multiple iterations of testing, a subset of claims (conditions) was used that
reflected the distribution of gender, age, language, and disability status present in full-year
submission data. The GBA+ analysis examined potential differential impacts on equity-
denied groups, including women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, and
members of 2SLGBTQI+ communities, with particular attention to persons with disabilities
given the nature of Disability Benefits programs.
The GBA+ analysis did not identify material differential adverse impacts or discriminatory
outcomes for equity-denied groups. Findings indicate that the CST is expected to improve
productivity and processing times, resulting in improved service delivery to clients.
Specifically, the CST is expected to support faster access to Disability Benefit decisions
and to the associated financial benefits and supports.
Over the longer term, improved workload management through the use of modernized and
automated tools is expected to increase Veterans Affairs Canada’s capacity to capture
and analyze client and program demographic data, including GBA+ data. Enhanced
availability and quality of demographic information is a key enabling factor for monitoring
equity outcomes and ensuring equitable access to programs and benefits. As a result,
equity deserving Veteran groups are expected to benefit from more timely processing of
Disability Benefit applications, especially in light of the evolving demographic profile of
Canadian Armed Forces personnel.

31. Describe how user feedback informs updates to the system.

review. User feedback is then reviewed and prioritized for inclusion in future development

32. Is there a recourse process established for clients to challenge the decision?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
33. Provide information on the recourse process or link to a website where this is
described.
The recourse system available to Veterans remains unchanged. The CST does not alter,
limit, or replace any existing review or appeal rights. Disability Benefits decisions continue
to be governed by a well-established legislative framework, including the Pension Act, the
Veterans Well-being Act, and the Veterans Review and Appeal Board Act, which together
provide multiple levels of recourse to support fairness, transparency, and procedural
integrity.
Veterans retain full access to all existing recourse mechanisms, including:
• Departmental Review – A review that may be initiated either by Veterans Affairs
Canada or by the Veteran.
• Veterans Review and Appeal Board (VRAB) – Independent reviews and appeals of
departmental decisions.
• Judicial Review – Applications for review to the Federal Court of Canada and the
Federal Court of Appeal.
Veterans also continue to have access to the Bureau of Pensions Advocates, which
provides free legal assistance to Veterans in preparing submissions and navigating the
review and appeal process.
Additional information about the appeal and review process: How to review or appeal a
decision
(Full URL: https:// www.veterans.gc.ca/en/veterans-rights/how-appeal )

37. Describe the metrics used and any mitigations taken to address identified issues.
The Department will monitor the performance of the CST over time using a combination of
accuracy-based and distributional metrics. Monitoring is conducted at an aggregate level
and, where appropriate, across relevant subgroups to identify potential performance
issues or unintended effects.
Human decision makers retain full authority for Disability Benefits determinations and are
responsible for reviewing CST outputs as part of the adjudication process. Decision
makers are required to use the Tool as an input to their review, while retaining the ability to
conduct additional examination of the full application record where clarification or
verification is required. This human-in-the-loop approach ensures that summaries
support, rather than replace, professional judgment.
Where monitoring or operational feedback identifies potential performance issues or risks,
mitigation measures may include refining prompts or model parameters, enhancing quality
assurance and guidance, increasing targeted human review, or implementing other
corrective actions in accordance with established governance and oversight processes.

48. Indicate the following in your answer: Title and scope of the PIA; How the

The Privacy Impact Assessment for the Disability Benefits Program was conducted to
support the introduction of the CST, which is used in the delivery of the Disability Benefits
program. The CST does not introduce the collection of new personal information, but it
processes personal information that is collected by Veterans Affairs Canada for the
administration of the program as outlined in the Disability Benefits Privacy Impact
Assessment and all relevant Personal Information Banks, and as authorized by the Pension
Act and the Veterans Well-being Act. The Privacy Impact Assessment was completed in
February 2026.

50. Describe
Page 31 of 32
Security controls were incorporated from the concept stage of the project and applied
throughout design and implementation. Access to CST summaries is restricted through
role-based access controls managed via Active Directory assignments, ensuring that only
authorized users can view summaries. PDF copies of summaries are stored within
Veterans Affairs Canada’s approved Digital Images database, which is itself access-
restricted to specific user groups.
Security controls were reviewed and validated through the completion of a
Security Statement of Authorization. Operational approval was granted because adequate
security controls have been applied and residual risks have been assessed and reduced to
an acceptable level in accordance with departmental security requirements.
